family_audit.reason: Round 20 measured the aggressive 256x128 pivot directly and closed this exact branch for now. The hot-band kernel ballooned to 43.10 us, tensor activity fell to 36.77%, register occupancy fell back to limit=1, and end-to-end runtime regressed by 5.964800 ms.

The active PTX-local control family has already spent enough budget for the current policy. Its measured changes live in the sub-0.1 ms band and are now treated as noise unless they shift counters materially, which they did not.
The grouped_rows=8 surface is already directly measured negative at 24.534016 ms and should not stay in the top set just because the current source is bad.

This remains the cleanest alternate hot-band surface with real evidence behind it. The grouped-row non-PTX sibling previously landed at 24.180737 ms with a hot-band kernel time of 32.697 us, effectively in the same band as the PTX winner.
The restore family is still the right low-risk re-anchor after a broad negative branch, but it should sit behind one evidence-backed alternate family rather than consuming every round.

Older round-history states in this family were not ready to win, but they were one of the few branches that actually changed the machine state in the direction the user wants: lower live-state pressure, more active warps, and higher theoretical ceiling. It belongs back in the live queue.
This family never got close enough to the current PTX band to outrank the sibling surface or the rehydrated high-ceiling half-panel branch.

directions.name: Port Grouped-Row Traversal Into The Non-PTX 128x128 Sibling
directions.hypothesis: Round 20 proved that the current aggressive move should not be another blind 256x128 geometry promotion: the hot-band kernel itself regressed from the PTX winner's 32.687 us to 43.097 us while tensor activity dropped from 48.39% to 36.77% and register occupancy collapsed from limit=2 back to limit=1. The best next move is therefore not another tiny PTX-local retime, but the closest evidence-backed alternate surface already in history: the grouped-row non-PTX 128x128 sibling. That branch previously measured 24.180737 ms with a 32.697 us hot-band kernel, essentially tied with the PTX winner inside the current noise band, while still changing the control/export surface materially enough to qualify as a real alternate family rather than a restore.
directions.expected_bottleneck: PTX-microkernel-specific control/export coupling on the current winner surface, while preserving grouped-row locality and the same broad 128x128 footprint.
directions.risk: Medium. This is a real surface pivot, but it is a measured-correct one that already stayed inside the 24.16-24.18 ms band instead of opening another multi-millisecond regression.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.164272 ms PTX best and the 30.136320 ms round-20 source run
ranking_notes: This is the highest-value next move because it recovers from the current bad 256x128 pivot state without spending the entire round on a pure restore.
Its prior measured runtime is already within the current sub-0.1 ms noise policy relative to the PTX winner, so it is a credible alternate branch rather than a speculative one.
It also keeps the implementation aggressive enough to satisfy the new guidance: this is a genuine execution-surface change, not another PTX-local hundredth-of-a-millisecond retime.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the sibling surface lands clearly outside the 24.16-24.18 ms band or if the hot-band kernel no longer tracks the PTX winner within a comparable utilization/stall envelope.

directions.name: Restore The Best Measured PTX Grouping Window On The Accepted Surface
directions.hypothesis: If the loop wants the fastest low-risk recovery from the current 30.136320 ms run, the direct answer is still to restore the exact 489574e PTX winner surface. That path measured 24.164272 ms, kept the hot-band kernel at 32.687 us, ran tensor at 48.39%, and held register occupancy at limit=2 with only 22,016 B of shared memory per block. It ranks second rather than first only because round 21 should spend one budget slot on a real alternate family before falling back to another re-anchor, not because the restore is weak.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The round-20 regression is dominated by the bad 256x128 pivot surface itself, so the restore attacks source drift rather than a live machine bottleneck family.
directions.risk: Low. This is the exact best measured surface and the cleanest way to erase the current 6 ms regression.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness pass rate across all 3 cases
ranking_notes: This stays near the top because the current source is objectively bad and the restore is the fastest way back to the real winner.
It is not ranked first because the user explicitly wants more aggressive post-selection moves than endless re-anchors.
If dir_01 fails to stay in the PTX winner band, this becomes the immediate next recovery action.
directions.stop_condition: Stop once the exact PTX winner surface is re-established or if the restored run no longer reproduces the prior 24.16-24.18 ms band.

directions.name: Repair The 256x128 Half-Panel Register-Reuse Branch With Compact B Staging
directions.hypothesis: Most aggressive 256x128 branches have failed for ordinary reasons and should stay closed, but one archived family is still different enough to keep alive: the half-panel register-reuse branch from the older round-history. Its historical implementations were not shippable, yet they did produce the kind of machine-state change the current search otherwise lacks: registers dropped sharply, active warps rose toward the 30%+ range, and the branch clearly attacked the live-state wall instead of just nudging barrier and scoreboard by tenths. The failure was not that the idea lacked headroom; it was that the implementation stopped halfway, still staging a full-width B tile and relying on implicit export mapping. Under the new guidance, that is exactly the kind of promising historical state that should be rehydrated into the live queue.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Register-limited occupancy and oversized live state on the wide hot-band family, with the real fix requiring end-to-end half-panel staging rather than only half-width accumulation.
directions.risk: High. This is a historical repair branch, not a bounded replay, and correctness plus feed-path integrity are both part of the work.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness pass rate across all 3 cases
ranking_notes: This is not ranked first because it is a real repair project, not a one-hunk probe.
It still belongs in the top three because it is one of the few historical families that actually moved occupancy and live-state pressure in the right direction.
Adding it back now satisfies the user's request to repopulate the live search queue with promising historical states after the search-policy reset.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the repaired branch cannot preserve correctness while keeping the lower-register, higher-active-warps signature that justified reopening it.

notes: Round 21 treats round 20 as a hard negative on the exact 256x128 pivot promotion, not as a reason to retreat into more PTX-local noise chasing. The new ranking therefore promotes the closest evidence-backed alternate surface, keeps the exact PTX restore as the low-risk recovery fallback, and rehydrates one historical high-ceiling half-panel family back into the live queue so the search does not stay trapped inside 24.16-24.18 ms micro-variance.
